r1_inhabitant: 天 (Tiān, Heaven) — the ultimate ground that exists prior to all things. Analects 8.19: 唯天為大 ('only Heaven is great'); Zhongyong 1: 天命之謂性 ('what Heaven mandates is called nature') — Heaven is presupposed as the giver, prior to all that is given.
r2_inhabitant: 天 / 誠 (Tiān / Chéng) — Heaven is the invariant background. Analects 17.19: 天何言哉？四時行焉，百物生焉 ('Does Heaven speak? Yet the four seasons run their course and the hundred creatures arise'). Zhongyong 26 specifies the invariance directly: 至誠無息 ('utmost sincerity has no cessation') and 天地之道...其為物不貳 ('the Way of Heaven and Earth...is single, not double'). 誠 functions here as the temporal-invariance attribute of 天.
r3_inhabitant: 性 (Xìng, nature) mandated by 天. Zhongyong 1: 天命之謂性 — the same Heaven-mandate constitutes the nature of every being. Mencius 6A.6: 仁義禮智，非由外鑠我也，我固有之也 ('humaneness, rightness, propriety, wisdom are not melted into me from outside; I inherently have them'). Mencius 2A.6 grounds this empirically with the child-at-the-well thought experiment — every human shows 不忍人之心 ('the heart that cannot bear another's suffering'). This is a strong R₃ fit: the same C present in every being's starting state.
r4_inhabitant: 誠 (Chéng, sincerity/actualization) with 仁 (Rén) as the relational mode. Zhongyong 25: 誠者非自成己而已也，所以成物也 ('sincerity is not merely self-completion but the completion of things') — sincerity sources outward production. Analects 6.30: 夫仁者，己欲立而立人 ('the humane person, wishing to stand, makes others stand'). Zhongyong 22: 能盡其性...則可以贊天地之化育 ('one who fully realizes nature can assist Heaven and Earth's transforming nourishment'). HONEST CAVEAT: Confucianism lacks the John 12:24 sacrificial-grain figure; sourcing here is generative cultivation, not death-then-fruit.
r5_inhabitant: 誠 / 仁 — inexhaustible by classical formulation. Zhongyong 26: 至誠無息，不息則久 ('utmost sincerity does not cease; not ceasing, it endures'). Mencius 7A.4: 萬物皆備於我矣。反身而誠，樂莫大焉 ('all things are complete in me; turning inward and being sincere, no joy is greater'). The junzi who gives 仁 is not depleted by giving; the source replenishes through the same act of giving.
r6_inhabitant: 天 — invisible, inferred from effects. Analects 17.19: Heaven does not speak; the seasons reveal it. Zhongyong 1: 莫見乎隱，莫顯乎微 ('nothing more visible than the hidden, nothing more manifest than the subtle'); Zhongyong 33 (closing) cites Shi Jing: 上天之載，無聲無臭 ('Heaven's workings have no sound and no scent'). This is exactly the kernel's T₃ recovery-by-subtraction — Heaven is read from the patterns of the ten-thousand-things, never as one of them.
r7_inhabitant: 天 / 命 / 性 — pre-input. Zhongyong 1 establishes the chain: 天命之謂性，率性之謂道，修道之謂教 ('what Heaven mandates is nature; following nature is the Way; cultivating the Way is teaching'). 性 is given before any cultivation (教); 命 is appointed before any action; 天 is prior to all. Mencius 6A.6 is explicit: the four sprouts are inherent, not acquired.
bridge_name: 天 (Tiān) — with 誠 (Chéng) as Heaven's mode of operation in the human, and 性 (Xìng) as Heaven's mandate distributed across all reasoners. 天 is the primary R₁/R₂/R₆/R₇ inhabitant; 誠 is the R₄/R₅ inhabitant; 性 is the R₃ vehicle. They form one cascade, not three entities.
scripture_anchor: Zhongyong (中庸) 1: 天命之謂性，率性之謂道，修道之謂教 — 'What Heaven mandates is called nature; following nature is called the Way; cultivating the Way is called teaching.' This single line maps Heaven (R₁/R₇) → mandate (R₃ universality) → nature (R₃ shared substrate) → Way (R₄ sourcing) → teaching (∫input). Plus Analects 17.19 for R₆ and Zhongyong 26 (至誠無息) for R₂/R₅.
honest_caveat: Three points of strain. (1) R₄ sourcing is strained: Confucianism has no analog to John 12:24's grain-of-wheat sacrificial economy. 誠's self-extension produces 物 but is not framed as a death-and-fruit dynamic; it is more like flowering than seeding. (2) 天 in classical Ru oscillates between an impersonal cosmic order and a quasi-personal mandator; Xunzi (荀子, 天論) explicitly naturalizes it (天行有常，不為堯存，不為桀亡 — 'Heaven's course is constant; it does not exist for Yao or perish for Jie'). This weakens the personal-God identification even while preserving R₁–R₇ structurally. (3) No single Confucian term fills all seven roles; the bridge requires a cascade (天 → 命 → 性 → 道 → 誠 → 仁), which is honest to Zhongyong 1 but more diffuse than 'God' or 'Brahman' as a single referent. The kernel's lemma still goes through because the cascade has one head (天); but the bridge name is properly a constellation, not a point.
notes: Sign(C) = positive is the Mencius branch (性善, human nature is good — Mencius 6A). Xunzi's 性惡 branch ('human nature is bad') is not a sign-flip on C but a re-description of the post-input self; even Xunzi accepts that the sages and the rites originate from a real cultivable ground. Uniqueness of 天 is given by Mencius 5A.4: 天無二日，民無二王 ('Heaven has not two suns, the people not two kings') — the Shema-pattern in Ru language. Compared to the existing Daoist witness: Daoism's 道 maps more cleanly to a single bridge name; Confucianism's strength is R₃ universality (性 mandated to all via 天命) and R₅ inexhaustibility (至誠無息); its weakness is R₄ (no sacrificial figure). The Christian 'C = love' identification is reachable via 仁 (humaneness, often translated 'benevolence' but operationally close to agape's outflow); Mencius 7B.16 says 仁也者，人也 ('humaneness is the human'), echoing 1 John 4:8's identity formula. T_bridge holds modulo the noted strain on R₄.
